data.title: Market Chatter: China New Home Prices Barely Fall; Analysts 'Less Bearish' on Housing Sector
data.description:
New home prices in China barely moved downward in January for the fifth straight month, signaling the start of stability in pricing, Bloomberg News reported Wednesday.

Prices of new homes in 70 cities declined 0.07% in January, slower than the 0.08% fall seen in December 2024, according to data from the National Bureau of Statistics.

Second-hand home prices declined 0.34% in January compared with 0.31% in December, the report said.

The narrower decline could be seen as a sign of hope for the struggling property sector, the report said.

However, China will need a solid rebound in prices to put a floor to declining prices, Bloomberg reported, citing a note by Fitch Ratings.

Other analysts are turning "less bearish on the sector" from February with better-than-expected home sales from the beginning of January and an increase in homebuyer visits, the report said, citing analysts at Citigroup.

(Market Chatter news is derived from conversations with market professionals globally. This information is believed to be from reliable sources but may include rumor and speculation. Accuracy is not guaranteed.)

data.title: Wheaton Precious Metals' FY24 Gold, Silver Production Exceeds Guidance Range
data.description:
Wheaton Precious Metals said Wednesday its gold and silver production for 2024 rose year over year and exceeded its guidance range.

Gold production totaled 379,742 ounces, compared with 374,585 ounces a year ago. Silver production came in at 20.7 million ounces, compared with 17.2 million ounces in 2023.

Wheaton's 2024 production guidance for gold was between 325,000 ounces and 370,000 ounces and the silver production guidance was between 18.5 million ounces and 20.5 million ounces.

For 2025, the Canadian precious metals streaming company expects gold production of between 350,000 ounces and 390,000 ounces, and silver production is expected in the range of 20.5 million ounces to 22.5 million ounces.

Wheaton is also targeting a 40% increase in production to 870,000 gold equivalent ounces by 2029.

data.title: Hainan Haiyao Subsidiary Gains Approval for Fengliao Stomach Health Tablet License
data.description:
Hainan Haiyao wholly-owned subsidiary, Haikou Pharmaceutical Factory, received approval from China's National Medical Products Administration to become the marketing authorization holder for Fengliao Stomach Health Dispersible Tablets, according to a Tuesday filing with the Shenzhen Bourse.

The drug, used for digestive issues like acute gastroenteritis, remains unchanged in the formula and production site. This approval strengthens the company's product lineup and market competitiveness.

data.title: News Highlights: Top Global Markets News of the Day - Wednesday at 3 AM ET
data.description:
Stocks Edge Higher; Intel Shares Rally

U.S. stocks rose slightly to start the week. Intel shares jumped after The Wall Street Journal reported that Broadcom and TSMC were eyeing separate deals that would break the U.S. chip maker in two.

Trump Floats 25% Tariffs on Autos, Chips, Pharmaceuticals

The president provided further details about his expected tariff moves.

U.K. Inflation Reaches 10-Month High

Consumer prices were 3.0% higher in January than a year earlier, up from 2.5% in December, complicating the picture for the BOE after it said it would cut interest rates gradually.

China Wants Its Economy to Grow 5%. Consumers Aren't Buying It.

Chinese consumers are nervous about the political and economic environments. Their pocketbooks are shut.

China Home Prices Show Slight Improvement But Remain in Decline

Home prices in major Chinese cities continued to slightly narrow their declines, suggesting that Beijing's policy blitz has helped stabilize the beleaguered real-estate sector.

BOJ's Takata Calls for Gradual Rate Hikes, Sees Upside Risks to Prices

The Bank of Japan needs to keep adjusting the degree of monetary accommodation in stages, policy board member Hajime Takata said as he warned about inflation risks.

RBNZ Slashes Rates Further, Sounds Global Trade Warning

The Reserve Bank of New Zealand slashed interest rates by a further 50 basis points on Wednesday, continuing its campaign to restart a stalled economy.

Fed Minutes Should Give More Clues About Rate Cut Timing

Minutes from the Federal Open Market Committee's Jan. 28-29 meeting will be released Wednesday afternoon at 2 p.m. Eastern time.

Howard Lutnick Is Confirmed as Commerce Secretary With Tariff Fights Heating Up

The billionaire Wall Street veteran is joining an economic team that includes a trade hawk, Peter Navarro.

U.S., Russia Forge New Path on Ukraine After First High-Level Talks

The announcement of a formal process to try to halt the Ukraine war followed talks in which Washington attempted to reset its tense relationship with Moscow.

data.title: News Highlights: Top Company News of the Day - Wednesday at 3 AM ET
data.description:
HSBC Posts Quarterly Net Profit, Plans $2 Billion Share Buyback

The London-based bank booked net profit of $197 million for the three months ended December, beating estimates by Visible Alpha.

Glencore Earnings Fall; Plans $2.2 Billion in Shareholder Returns

Glencore's earnings declined on lower coal prices, missing market views, but the mining and trading giant still plans to return around $2.2 billion to shareholders.

Apple's Big Launch Could Bring a Cheaper iPhone

CEO Tim Cook promised a new product today, and Wall Street is expecting a new version of its iPhone SE, which could boost sales in China.

Shift4 Strikes $1.5 Billion Deal for Tax-Free Shopping Business

The expected $7.50-per-share offer for Global Blue Group represents a 15% premium to where the tax-free shopping services provider's shares closed last week.

Dell expected to provide lower guidance next week, but AI servers remain a big bright spot

Here's why one analyst advises, "Don't bet against Michael Dell."

Bumble Shares Tumble on Weak Outlook, Rebound Efforts Continue

The online-dating company posted operating earnings of $37 million, compared with an operating loss of $6.9 million a year earlier.

Activist Launches Proxy Fight at National Health Investors

Land & Buildings argues the REIT is too closely tied to a large tenant and could get better lease terms.

Toll Brothers Misses Earnings Estimates. The Stock Falls.

Earnings that came in below estimates were due to a delay in the sale of a stabilized apartment property, Toll Brothers said.

Ackman Boosts Bid for Howard Hughes, Vowing to Create Modern Version of Berkshire Hathaway

The revised proposal would increase Pershing Square's stake to 48%.

Air Canada Expects Several Days Before Normalized Service Following Storms, Toronto Crash

The airline said it is restoring service and adding extra flights and capacity where possible but that the reduction in the number of take-offs and landings permitted at its global hub in Toronto is slowing its recovery.

data.title: Telefonica Plans to Raise Up to $2.82 Billion Through Divestments, Expansion Says
data.description:
• Telefonica plans to raise between 2.50 billion euros and 2.70 billion euros ($2.61 billion and $2.82 billion) through different asset divestment processes currently underway in Europe and Latin America, Spanish newspaper Expansion reports, without citing sources.
  • The transactions located in Argentina and Colombia would involve the sale of all subsidiaries based in each of these countries, Expansion says.
  • The group is also considering the sale of minority stakes in fiber or fixed cable telecommunications networks in the U.K. and Spain, the newspaper adds.

Full story: https://tinyurl.com/52m24unz

Write to Najat Kantouar at najat.kantouar@wsj.com

data.title: Sibanye-Stillwater Agrees Enhancement to Historical Marikana Contract
SGL Carbon Shares Trade 1.8% Lower After Decision to Reconstruct Carbon Fibres Business Unit
Julius Baer Has Narrow Range of Options for Strategy — Market Talk
data.description: Julius Baer has a relatively narrow range of options to implement its new strategy, prompting Jefferies to downgrade its rating on the stock to hold from buy, the broker says in a research note. "New CEO, Stefan Bollinger, deserves an open-minded assessment of his strategic vision for the firm...but we prefer to approach it from a neutral stance," analysts write. The initial comments by the Swiss bank's new boss suggest his outlook will be based on a client focus, technology, disciplined entrepreneurship, its performance and ownership-led culture and ambition to be a prime spot for talent, they say. These seem "perfectly reasonable maxims" but cost actions might still be needed to bring its cost-to-income ratio down. The share price now reflects its incremental cost-efficiency measures and lack of near-term share buyback, they write. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

data.title: Infosys, Lufthansa Partner to Set Up Global Aviation Center in Bengaluru, India.
data.description:
Indian technology services provider Infosys has entered into a strategic deal with Lufthansa Group and its IT subsidiary, Lufthansa Systems GmbH, to accelerate digital transformation in the aviation industry, according to a Tuesday filing to the Indian stock exchanges.

As part of the long-term partnership, the companies will establish a dedicated global capability center in Bengaluru, India.

The new center is aimed to help boost Lufthansa Group's software and product development as well as increase support for Lufthansa Systems products for the external airline market customers.

Shares of the company were down over 1% in recent trade.

data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0752 GMT - The Bank of England is in for a nasty headache if rising wages continue to keep price inflation running high and the U.K. economy struggles to gain any momentum, Quilter Cheviot's Richard Carter writes in a note to clients. Consumer prices rose 3% in January, driven by higher inflation in services and energy, figures show Wednesday, a day after separate data showed U.K. wage growth continues to gain pace. The BOE has shifted its focus to weak growth and looks determined to see through a likely spike in inflation, Carter says. But if the road to lower price rises proves bumpier than hoped, the central bank will have a tough task on its hand, he says. (joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

0745 GMT - The Singapore government's projection of a surplus in fiscal 2025 is likely to cushion against global uncertainties, according to CGS International economists in a note. They suggest that the government may have factored in the possibility that GDP growth and revenue outlooks could become uncertain if global conditions worsen. "This could prompt the authorities to embark on more expansionary policies to mitigate their impact while still keeping the fiscal position healthy," they add. CGS International maintains its forecast for Singapore's 2025 GDP growth at 2.5%. (amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0735 GMT - Bank of Japan policy board member Hajime Takata's speech suggests that he may favor hiking interest rates at a faster pace, depending on economic data and financial market developments, says BNP Paribas economist Ryutaro Kono. Kono says it's noteworthy that Takata mentioned the risk of financial overheating in his speech, in addition to the possibility of prices picking up faster than expected. Kono expects the BOJ to raise the policy rate in June, but thinks that it could pull its decision forward to the April 30-May 1 meeting. Takata's comments "clearly signal a positive stance on continuing to raise interest rates," Kono says.(megumi.fujikawa@wsj.com)

0730 GMT - Sterling is little moved even after data showed U.K. inflation accelerated more than expected in January. Inflation increased to 3.0% year-on-year in January from 2.5% in December. Economists in a WSJ survey expected 2.8%. Core inflation rose to 3.7% from 3.2% a month ago, as expected. The introduction of VAT on private-school fees, rising fuel prices, air fares and bus fares were "always likely to drive prices up," Raymond James Investment Services strategist Jeremy Batstone-Carr says in a note. The Bank of England could view rising inflation as temporary but Wednesday's data suggest the next interest rates cut is some way off, he says. GBP/USD trades at 1.2618 and EUR/GBP is at 0.8286, little changed after the data. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0727 GMT - Inflation rose faster than expected at the start of the year but the Bank of England is unlikely to be deterred from its plans for gradual rate-cuts this year, writes Joe Nellis, economic adviser to consultancy MHA. Consumer prices rose 3% on year in January, picking up more pace than economists had estimated, figures show Wednesday. But that acceleration was down to regulated prices--such as energy and value-added taxes--rather than underlying pressures, Nellis notes. "A close watch will be kept on price rises for some months ahead, but the Bank will be content knowing that sustainable inflationary pressures remain cooled," he tells clients. "At least for now."(joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

0715 GMT - German 10-year Bund yields look stable around 2.50%, while narrowing French OAT and Italian BTP spreads over Bunds signal hopes of joint European debt issuance, says Commerzbank Research's Michael Leister in a note. "The direction of travel remains clear as the ongoing bid in BTPs and OATs at the cyclical lows versus Bunds signals mutualization hopes," the head of interest rates strategy says. Meanwhile, the EU is considering to boost defense budgets in an effort to continue to support Ukraine. The 10-year OAT-Bund yield spread is 67 basis points, the lowest since late July, while the 10-year BTP-Bund yield spread is just below 106 basis points, according to Tradeweb. The 10-year Bund yield is up 1 basis point at 2.493%. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0709 GMT - Europe's zippy stock market might point to hope that current travails will catalyse bolder action on the continent, Pantheon Macroeconomics' Claus Vistesen and Melanie Debono write in a note. The Stoxx Europe 600 has hit new heights this year, driven by defense stocks amid plans for a step-up in military spending. That buoyancy might seem odd given Europe's fiscal, political and trade problems, Vistesen and Debono say. But those challenges might precisely be so great that investors see drastic action as the only option in Europe's capitals, they say. "[B]etting on Europe to get its act together means betting on stronger economic and political integration, and fiscal stimulus," the economists say. "Lots of fiscal stimulus." (joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

0706 GMT - Japanese stocks ended lower as concerns about borrowing costs and U.S. tariffs persisted. The Nikkei Stock Average fell 0.3% to 39164.61. Auto stocks and trading houses led declines. Honda Motor shed 2.3% and Toyota Motor fell 1.7% after President Trump said he was considering tariffs of 25% or more on automobiles. Itochu dropped 2.6% and Mitsubishi Corp. declined 1.8%. The 10-year Japanese government bond yield rose 0.5 basis point to 1.435%, the highest level since November 2009. U.S. tariff and foreign policies are in focus. USD/JPY is at 151.63, compared with 152.06 as of Tuesday 5 p.m. Eastern time. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com; @kosakunarioka)

0659 GMT - The car industry is back in focus after President Trump said he was considering tariffs of 25% on autos, semiconductors and pharmaceuticals. Analysts at Nomura noted that the U.S. is the largest export market outside of the eurozone for German car manufacturers, and Trump has taken particular issue with the European automotive sector and Europeans not buying U.S. cars. "Some car manufacturers already forewarned factory closures and layoffs prior to Trump's election in November 2024, and the prospect of U.S. tariffs only worsens the situation," they write. Nomura also expects the U.S. administration to apply reciprocal tariffs sometime in April. Currently, the U.S. has a 2.5% tariff on European cars, while the EU charges U.S. imports 10%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0655 GMT - U.S. fiscal policy is core to Deutsche Bank Research's rates forecasts for this year, its strategists say in a note. They expect a modest net fiscal easing but with unusually elevated uncertainty related to both taxes and spending, they say. Currently, however, they don't yet see anything in the data that suggests federal spending has been put on a significantly different trajectory. Ten-year U.S. Treasury yields trade within their recent range, last little changed at 4.546%, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0652 GMT - South Korea's benchmark Kospi rose 1.7% to close at 2671.52, extending gains for a seventh consecutive session. Semiconductor and battery stocks led the gains. Foreign and institutional investors were net buyers. Chip stock gains on Wall Street overnight supported investor sentiment. Memory-chip makers Samsung Electronics and SK Hynix rose 3.2% and 4.0%, respectively. Samsung's planned share cancellation also kept the index-heavyweight stock higher for a second consecutive session. Electric-vehicle battery maker LG Energy Solution added 4.4%. USD/KRW settled 0.4% lower at 1,438.50 in Seoul onshore trading. South Korea's 10-year government bond yield was up 0.1 basis point at 2.879%. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

0649 GMT - The improved investor assessment seen in Germany's ZEW sentiment index released Tuesday in part "reflects increased expectations of ongoing European Central Bank rate cuts," say Daiwa Capital Markets Research analysts. Rate cuts are likely to go "perhaps into accommodative territory," they say in a note. Investors' expectations for the next six months rose 15.7 points on the month in February to 26.0, the indicator's biggest increase in two years. "And with luck, that improved investor mood will translate itself into a pickup in business survey indicators, such as the Ifo indices as well as firmer demand in the real economy," they added. Money markets price in three more ECB rate cuts of 25 bps each this year, bringing the deposit rate to 2.00% by September, according to LSEG. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

data.title: Bank of England Faces Tough Task as Inflation Rises, Economy Struggles — Market Talk
data.description: The Bank of England is in for a nasty headache if rising wages continue to keep price inflation running high and the U.K. economy struggles to gain any momentum, Quilter Cheviot's Richard Carter writes in a note to clients. Consumer prices rose 3% in January, driven by higher inflation in services and energy, figures show Wednesday, a day after separate data showed U.K. wage growth continues to gain pace. The BOE has shifted its focus to weak growth and looks determined to see through a likely spike in inflation, Carter says. But if the road to lower price rises proves bumpier than hoped, the central bank will have a tough task on its hand, he says. (joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

data.title: BAE Systems Posts Double-Digit Jump in Sales as Defense Budgets Climb
data.description:
By Cristina Gallardo

BAE Systems posted double-digit growth in sales in 2024, boosted by growing defense budgets in its main markets.

The London-listed aerospace and defense manufacturer said Wednesday that sales reached 28.335 billion pounds ($35.74 billion) last year, up 14% on a constant currency basis from 25.28 billion pounds in 2023.

BAE, which makes a range of war equipment and provides cybersecurity services, said its order backlog reached 77.8 billion pounds as of the end of 2024, beating a Visible Alpha estimate of 74.45 billion pounds. The company's order intake was worth 33.7 billion pounds.

Underlying earnings before interest and taxes--the group's preferred measure of profitability--reached 3.015 billion pounds in 2024 compared with 2.68 billion pounds in 2023.

Net income stood at 1.96 billion pounds last year, up from 1.85 billion pounds the previous year, above a Visible Alpha consensus estimate of 1.95 billion pounds.

"Based on the exceptional visibility of our record order backlog and sustainability of our value-compounding business model, we remain confident in the positive momentum of our business into the future," Chief Executive Charles Woodburn said.

For 2025, BAE Systems expects sales to range between 29.17 billion and 31.59 billion pounds. Underlying EBIT should be between 3.26 billion and 3.50 billion pounds, it said.

The board declared a dividend of 20.6 pence, taking the total dividend to 33 pence a share, up 10% on year.

Write to Cristina Gallardo at cristina.gallardo@wsj.com

data.title: Royal Philips Forecasts Comparable Sales Growth in FY25
data.description:
Royal Philips said Wednesday it expects comparable sales growth in the full year 2025 despite an anticipated slowdown in the first quarter.

The Dutch health technology company forecast a 1% to 3% comparable sales growth in the full year. Comparable sales for the first three months are expected to decrease by a mid-single-digit percentage amid lower demand in China.

data.title: BAE Systems 2024 Results Beat Consensus — Market Talk
data.description: BAE Systems' 2024 results beat market expectations, J.P. Morgan analyst David Perry says. The British aerospace and defense group reported earnings per share 3% ahead of Vuma consensus, while its free cash flow was 890 million pounds above consensus, he says. The company's evolution in 2026 should become clearer in the coming months, once the U.S. administration tables its 2026 defense budget in April, and the U.K. government sets out its military spending plans, Perry notes. "We recommend the shares as a long-term holding; however, after an 11% rally in the last five days we don't expect a major reaction today," he adds. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

data.title: Court Approves Rendong's Reorganization Plan
data.description:
Rendong Holdings will be able to implement the reorganization of its debt after its plan was approved by the Intermediate People's Court of Guangzhou City, Guangdong Province, China, according to a Shenzhen Stock Exchange filing on Tuesday.

An administrator will be appointed to manage the reorganization, the financial services provider said.

The company's shares closed 5% higher.

data.title: Maxis' Profit Soars 473% in Q4
data.description:
Maxis' profit attributable to owners rocketed 473% to 321 million ringgit in the fourth quarter of 2024, from 56 million ringgit a year earlier.

Earnings per share jumped to 0.041 ringgit versus 0.007 ringgit a year ago, according to a Tuesday Malaysian bourse filing.

Revenue improved to 2.77 billion ringgit during the period from 2.74 billion ringgit a year earlier, the Malaysian telecommunications company said.

data.title: Glencore's Earnings Fall; Plans $2.2 Billion in Shareholder Returns
data.description:
By Adam Whittaker

Glencore's earnings fell declined on lower coal prices, missing market views, but the mining and trading giant still plans to return around $2.2 billion to shareholders.

The commodities giant said Wednesday that adjusted earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization fell 16% to $14.36 billion. This compared with analysts' expectations of $14.61 billion, according to a consensus compiled by Visible Alpha.

Glencore said its results reflect the progressive normalization of energy markets after the severe disruptions seen over 2022-23 during the start of the Russian-Ukraine conflict.

Thermal coal and gas prices materially declined but the market for copper and zinc concentrates was favorable, it said.

Revenue rose 6% on year to $230.94 billion, compared with $232.93 billion analysts had expected.

The miner swung to a $1.63 billion net loss from $4.28 billion in profit a year earlier, as it booked $5.3 billion in impairments, including at its South African coal operations, the company said.

The miner announced around $2.2 billion in shareholder returns and said it will recommend a base dividend of $0.10 a share and launch a buyback of $1 billion.

Write to Adam Whittaker at adam.whittaker@wsj.com

data.title: British Pound Hover at 2-Month Highs
data.description:
The British pound fluctuated around $1.26, hovering near two-month highs as traders digest key economic data and its implications for monetary policy.

The annual inflation rate unexpectedly rose to 3% last month, the highest in ten months, exceeding forecasts of 2.8%.

Meanwhile, services inflation climbed to 5%, slightly below the Bank of England's projection of 5.2%.

With inflation moving further from the central bank's 2% target, the data reinforced the BoE's cautious stance on rate cuts.

Additionally, figures released on Tuesday showed wage growth accelerated in the final quarter of 2024, also rising in real terms when adjusted for inflation.

As a result, money markets now expect only two rate cuts by the BoE this year, bringing interest rates down to 4%.

data.title: Arkade Developers Bags Redevelopment Right for Co-operative Housing Society in Mumbai
data.description:
Arkade Developers (NSE:ARKADE; BOM:544261) secured redevelopment rights to a co-operative housing society named Nutan Ayojan in Mumbai, India, according to a Wednesday filing to the Indian stock exchanges.

The project is expected to generate a gross development value of around 7.40 billion Indian rupees.

The project will offer 408 homes, out of which around 215 units will be available for sale, further expanding its residential and commercial offerings.

data.title: Indonesia Central Bank Holds Rates as It Eyes Rupiah, Trade Risks
data.description:
By Ying Xian Wong

Indonesia's central bank hit the pause button again, holding rates steady after a surprise cut last month as it monitors currency headwinds and rising external risks.

Bank Indonesia kept its benchmark seven-day reverse repo rate at 5.75% on Wednesday, in a decision analysts had expected to be a close one. Five out of seven economists polled by The Wall Street Journal had forecast a hold, while two had projected a 25-basis-point cut.

The decision is aimed at keeping inflation on target, and supporting growth amid external uncertainties, Bank Indonesia Gov. Perry Warjiyo said at a press conference. The central bank also held its overnight deposit facility rate at 5.00% and its lending facility rate at 6.50%.

The move comes as global central banks express concerns about the unpredictability of policymaking under the Trump administration, and the risks posed to growth and trade. Indonesia, Southeast Asia's largest economy, is exposed to commodity price shocks, but looks more insulated against tariff risks than others in Asia thanks to its relatively smaller trade surplus with the U.S. and domestic-driven growth, analysts say.

Bank Indonesia's main concern is likely the rupiah, which has come under pressure amid trade uncertainty and dollar strength, depreciating about 1.4% versus the greenback year to date, according to LSEG data.

BI Gov. Warjiyo said Wednesday that the currency remains under control despite the rate cut in January, and has been strengthening in February, maintaining relative stability compared to the currencies of Indonesia's key trading partners.

More rate cuts could be in the pipeline, with the central bank having surprised markets in January by lowering rates despite an unsteady rupiah, but it remains to be seen what policymakers will prioritize.

DBS economist Radhika Rao believes Indonesia's monetary policy will need to do more of the heavy lifting in supporting growth this year, as the government's focus remains on fiscal consolidation amid efforts to raise revenue collections. That effort is in question after the government deferred a VAT rate increase and introduced stimulus measures to spur growth, part of which is being financed via a rationalization in spending, she said.

Indonesia's government has announced a multibillion-dollar stimulus package for 2025 as it looks to hit an ambitious 8% economic growth target.

Increased government spending could help boost household consumption, supporting economic growth, CIMB economists said in a recent note. Coupled with external uncertainties, signs of better growth could keep Bank Indonesia in wait-and-see mode, strategically reducing the policy rate only when a favorable window opens, they said.

If the currency remains stable, there's potential for further easing in the second quarter, Barclays economist Brian Tan said in a recent note. A wider trade surplus in January could also strengthen BI's confidence in managing external pressures and supports its growth-focused stance, he added.

Write to Ying Xian Wong at yingxian.wong@wsj.com

data.title: Glencore Reports Preliminary Loss in FY24; Revenue Up
data.description:
Glencore (, GLN.JO) on Wednesday reported a preliminary loss for full-year 2024, while revenue rose year over year.

Loss attributable to equity holders of the parent for the 12 months ended Dec. 31, 2024, was $1.63 billion, compared with a profit of $4.28 billion a year ago. Loss per share moved to $0.13 from an EPS of $0.34.

The mining giant's revenue was $230.94 billion, compared with $217.83 billion earlier.

data.title: Solana and XRP Prices Take a Tumble: What's Behind the Crypto Crash?
data.description:
Market turbulence hits Solana and XRP as scandals and regulatory uncertainties shake investor confidence.

Solana's Descent: Political Scandals and Memecoin Mayhem

Solana (SOL) has had a rough start to 2025, plunging more than 40% in just a month. The primary driver behind this decline? A perfect storm of political scandal, market sell-offs, and the lingering volatility of memecoins.

Reuters
@Reuters

In a new development, an Argentine judge was assigned to investigate President Javier Milei's role in promoting a cryptocurrency that crashed https://t.co/z9C0uJbzlB pic.twitter.com/k1ofHjKF1w

Feb 18, 2025

The trouble started when Argentine President Javier Milei openly endorsed LIBRA, a Solana-based memecoin that quickly became a cautionary tale in the crypto world. What initially seemed like a strong show of support from a sitting world leader turned into a nightmare when LIBRA’s market cap plummeted from $4.5 billion, losing 90% of its value. The project turned out to be a textbook pump-and-dump, leaving many retail investors empty-handed.

This spectacular collapse triggered a wave of sell-offs across the Solana network. Solana had already been riding high after a strong performance in late 2024, but the memecoin fiasco shattered investor confidence. Adding fuel to the fire, Milei himself is now facing legal action, with multiple lawsuits filed by Argentine lawyers alleging financial misconduct related to the LIBRA scandal.

Despite the chaos, some analysts remain optimistic, arguing that Solana’s fundamentals remain strong. VanEck, Bitwise, and Pantera Capital have all suggested that that SOL could still rally, with VanEck claiming it could reach to $520 by the end of 2025 if the current turbulence subsides and institutional interest remains strong.

XRP's Slump: Regulatory Rollercoaster and Political Letdowns

If Solana’s downfall was fueled by scandal, XRP’s woes stem from regulatory uncertainty and political letdowns.

X Screenshot, 9.40am, GMT+2, 19/02/25

XRP, which had a promising start to 2024, has dropped 3.6% to $2.59, marking a 19% decline over the past month. Much of this decline has been attributed to a mix of SEC-related concerns and fading optimism around crypto-friendly policies from the new Trump administration.

CryptoBull
@CryptoBull2020

Trump just posted about #XRP. I checked it out. It’s still there 🚀 pic.twitter.com/XTlg8MQJQM

Feb 19, 2025

One of the biggest hopes for XRP was the approval of an XRP Exchange-Traded Fund (ETF), which would have allowed institutional investors to gain exposure to XRP without directly buying the token. While analysts believe an approval could lead to huge inflows, the SEC has so far delayed any clear decision.

Adding to XRP’s troubles is the Trump administration’s unpredictable stance on crypto regulation. While candidate Trump hinted at potential pro-crypto policies—at one point even teasing a Bitcoin strategic reserve—his government’s actions so far have been lackluster. In fact, Trump’s new round of tariffs on Canada, Mexico, and China has triggered broader market fears, leading to a general risk-off sentiment in crypto.

The ETF Tease: Hope on the Horizon?

Despite the bearish sentiment, some crypto bulls argue that XRP and Solana still have a chance to recover, thanks to potential ETF approvals.

Amonyx
@amonbuy

💥JUST IN: 🇺🇸 SEC acknowledges filing for Bitwise spot $XRP ETF. pic.twitter.com/KVAygLPYrS

Feb 18, 2025

Regarding XRP, the SEC is reviewing multiple ETF applications from Grayscale, Bitwise and more, and if approved, institutional investors could potentially inject vast sums. High levels of liquidity could significantly reverse the current downward trajectory.

Solana, too, has ETF potential, with investors showing interest in bringing a regulated Solana fund to the market. If approved, an ETF could help restore confidence in the ecosystem and push Solana higher. However, given recent controversies, regulators may be hesitant to greenlight anything related to Solana in the near term.

Proceed with Caution

While some long-term investors remain hopeful, the short-term outlook for Solana and XRP remains volatile.

Solana’s price may continue to feel the aftershocks of the LIBRA scandal, especially if the lawsuits against Milei gain traction. If more memecoins on Solana collapse, investors may start questioning the network’s ability to support long-term, serious projects.

XRP, on the other hand, is still caught in the regulatory crossfire. The SEC’s stance on crypto remains unpredictable, and until XRP gets clear regulatory approval—particularly in the form of an ETF—its price may remain stagnant.

For now, crypto investors should brace for more turbulence. Between political scandals, regulatory hurdles, and global market jitters, the coming months could be make-or-break for Solana and XRP.

One thing is clear: crypto is never boring.

data.title: Bayer Receives FDA Endorsement to Accelerate Development of Parkinson Treatment
data.description:
By Helena Smolak

Bayer's subsidiary AskBio received approval from the U.S. drug regulator to accelerate the development of its investigational gene therapy to treat moderate-stage Parkinson's disease.

The German pharmaceutical company said Wednesday that its treatment received advanced therapy designation from the U.S. Food and Drug Administration after its early-stage study showed slowed disease progression and improved motor outcomes in patients after 36 months.

The study demonstrated a favorable safety profile and the experimental drug was well tolerated by patients, the company said.

Bayer is currently recruiting patients for its mid-stage study of the experimental drug in the U.S.

Write to Helena Smolak at helena.smolak@wsj.com

data.title: BHP Group Unit Prices US Offering of $3 Billion Bonds
data.description:
BHP Group (, BHG.JO) said Wednesday that its BHP Billiton Finance (USA) subsidiary priced a $3 billion senior unsecured bonds offering in the US.

The offer comprises $1 billion of 5% fixed-rate bonds due in 2030, $750 million of 5.125% fixed-rate bonds due in 2032, and $1.25 billion of 5.30% fixed-rate bonds due in 2035.

The mining company intends to use proceeds from the issuance for general corporate expenditures. Settlement is expected by Friday, subject to customary closing conditions.

data.title: Gansu Jiu Steel to Absorb Metal Products Trading Subsidiary
data.description:
Gansu Jiu Steel Group Hongxing Iron & Steel plans to absorb and merge with metal and mineral products trading subsidiary Urumqi Jiugang Zhengtai Logistics, according to a Shanghai Stock Exchange filing on Wednesday.

The Chinese iron and steel manufacturer will take over all of Jiugang Zhengtai Logistics' assets and liabilities.

Jiugang Zhengtai Logistics will be canceled after the completion of the merger.
